title: Reward Design: The Modeling Decision That Decides Everything
learningObjectives: Explain why reward design is a modeling decision, not an algorithmic detail
Combine multiple competing objectives (cost, emissions, overload risk) into a single scalar reward with justified weights
Recognize a reward function that would produce unintended 'reward hacking' behavior before training reveals it
Iterate on a reward function based on observed agent behavior rather than just intuition upfront
whyItMatters: The MDP you defined last lesson has a reward function, but a careless one — an RL agent will optimize exactly what you told it to, including any unintended shortcut your reward accidentally permits, and that 'accidentally permits' gap is where most real-world RL projects actually fail.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's actual reward function for battery scheduling — the single most consequential design decision in this entire skill folder, since every algorithm downstream will optimize exactly what this function rewards, nothing more and nothing less.

problem.summary: A cost-only reward ignores emissions and safety, producing the wrong kind of 'optimal'.
problem.content: Your reward function from the last lesson only accounts for cost. A real grid operator also cares about emissions and never wants the battery pushed into an unsafe state — and a reward that ignores those will train an agent that's technically 'optimal' by a definition nobody actually wanted.

motivation.summary: No algorithmic sophistication later fixes a reward rewarding the wrong behavior.
motivation.content: This is the single highest-leverage decision in reinforcement learning: an agent trained against the wrong reward will confidently, efficiently optimize for the wrong thing, and no amount of algorithmic sophistication in later lessons fixes a reward function that's rewarding the wrong behavior.

theory.summary: Weighted sums combine objectives; reward hacking exploits gaps between reward and true intent.
theory.content: Combining multiple objectives into one scalar reward typically means a weighted sum: `reward = -cost - w1 * emissions - w2 * overload_penalty`, where the weights (`w1`, `w2`) encode relative priority and require deliberate, documented justification, not arbitrary numbers. 'Reward hacking' is when an agent finds a way to maximize reward that technically satisfies the reward function but violates the actual intent — e.g. an agent that discovers it can avoid an overload penalty by simply never discharging the battery at all, technically 'safe' but useless. Reward design is inherently iterative: you define a reward, train an agent, observe its actual behavior, and revise the reward if the behavior reveals a gap your original definition didn't anticipate — this loop, not a single upfront definition, is the realistic process.

visualExplanation.summary: Weighted objective meters combine into one reward; a hacked path branches off exploiting a loophole.
visualExplanation.content: Three separate objective meters (cost, emissions, overload risk) feed into weighted arrows that combine into a single reward number, with an example of a 'hacked' reward path branching off showing an agent exploiting an unintended loophole.

interactiveDemo.summary: Adjust objective weights and watch a pre-trained agent's behavior shift, including discovering a hack.
interactiveDemo.content: Adjust the weights on cost/emissions/overload-penalty and watch a pre-trained agent's simulated behavior shift accordingly, including deliberately discovering a reward-hacking exploit at an extreme weight setting.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: combine three objectives into one weighted reward.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
def compute_reward(state, action_result, weights):
    cost_term = -action_result["cost"]
    emissions_term = -weights["emissions"] * action_result["emissions_kg"]
    overload_term = -weights["overload"] * action_result["overload_penalty"]
    return cost_term + emissions_term + overload_term

reward = compute_reward(state, action_result, weights={"emissions": 0.5, "overload": 10.0})
```

independentCoding.summary: Design and sanity-check your own weighted reward for a stated priority.
independentCoding.content: Design and justify your own weights for a three-objective reward (cost, emissions, overload risk) for a scenario where the grid operator has explicitly stated emissions matter twice as much as raw cost, and test it against a few hand-constructed scenarios to sanity-check the resulting reward values.

debugging.summary: Fix a reward where the overload penalty is too small relative to potential savings.
debugging.content: A reward's overload penalty is smaller in magnitude than the potential cost savings from risky behavior, meaning a rational agent would learn to accept occasional overloads to save money. Identify the magnitude mismatch and fix the weighting.

reflection.summary: A weight that permits unsafe behavior is a reward-design bug, not a code bug.
reflection.content: If your first reward function's weights turned out to permit occasional overloads because the penalty was too small, is that a bug in the code, or a bug in the reward design itself?

teachBack.summary: Explain 'reward hacking' with a concrete example.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone what 'reward hacking' means, using a concrete example of a reward an agent could technically satisfy while violating the actual intent.

miniProject.summary: Finalize the battery-scheduling reward function with documented iteration.
miniProject.content: Finalize the Digital Twin's battery-scheduling reward function combining cost, emissions, and a hard safety constraint, with a written justification for every weight chosen and at least one documented iteration where you revised a weight after spotting a potential exploit.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the actual specification of 'good behavior' every algorithm will optimize.
portfolioEvidence.content: This reward function is the actual specification of 'good behavior' every RL algorithm in the rest of this skill folder will be trained to satisfy.

labs.title: Multi-Objective Reward
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise designing and stress-testing a weighted multi-objective reward function, requiring identification of a planted magnitude-mismatch reward-hacking vulnerability.

simulations.title: Reward Weight & Hacking Visualizer
simulations.description: Adjustable objective-weight sliders showing a pre-trained agent's behavior shift, including a discoverable reward-hacking exploit at extreme settings.

flashcards.back: When an agent finds a way to maximize the reward function that technically satisfies it but violates the actual intent behind it — e.g. avoiding a penalty by never taking the risky action type at all, rather than actually managing the risk well.

flashcards.front: Why is reward design inherently iterative rather than a one-time upfront definition?
flashcards.back: You typically can't anticipate every gap between the reward function and the true intent in advance — training an agent and observing its actual behavior reveals exploits or omissions you revise the reward to close.

rubric: Every weight in the final reward function is justified with a specific, stated reason, not an arbitrary number
Correctly identifies at least one plausible reward-hacking vulnerability and revises the reward to close it
Documents at least one iteration where observed or hypothesized agent behavior led to a reward revision

references.title: DeepMind — Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity
